explicit_text: In March of 1943, after I turned 18, I was drafted and sent to Fort Oglethorpe, Georgia for my physical examinations and induction into the Army. We stayed there about a week while they processed us. It was my first step into military life, marking the beginning of my service during World War II.

explicit_text: After induction, I was sent to Camp Butner in North Carolina for six months of hard training. It was there that I trained with the 277th Field Artillery Regiment using 105 mm Howitzers. The training was intense but prepared us well for combat.

explicit_text: One of the most memorable and intense experiences was the Battle of the Bulge in December 1944. We were caught in the German breakthrough near St. Vith, Belgium, just 15 miles from Bastogne. The fighting was brutal, and we were ordered to destroy our guns at one point but then told to hold them. It was a harsh winter with freezing conditions that caused many injuries.

explicit_text: During the Battle of the Bulge, a German unit captured about 110 to 115 American soldiers and took them to Malmedy. In freezing weather, the Germans executed most of them in what became known as the Malmedy massacre. Years later, in 1985, my wife and I visited the memorial site in Germany where their names are inscribed on a circular stone wall.
